questions.question: The number of possible commutative binary operations that can be defined on a set of n elements (for a given n) is ___________.
questions.to_find: Count the commutative binary operations on an n-element set.

understand.plain: A binary operation on a set S of n elements is just a function that takes an ordered pair (a, b) and returns one of the n elements - so it is fully described by filling an n x n operation table. Commutativity means a*b = b*a, so the table is symmetric across its main diagonal; once you fill the diagonal and the upper triangle, the lower triangle is forced. So you only freely choose values for the n diagonal cells plus the C(n,2) cells above it, and each of those n(n+1)/2 cells can independently be any of the n elements.

keywords.explain: a rule * : S x S -> S that assigns to every ordered pair a single element of S
keywords.example: on {a,b} the table has 4 cells, each filled with a or b

keywords.explain: the order of the two inputs does not matter: a*b = b*a for all a,b
commutativity makes the operation table mirror across the diagonal, so only the diagonal and upper triangle are free choices
keywords.example: for n=3 the free cells are 3 (diagonal) + 3 (upper) = 6

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 560 250' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' role='img' aria-label='Free cells of a symmetric operation table'><title>Free cells of a symmetric operation table</title><desc>For a commutative operation the n x n table is symmetric, so only the diagonal plus the upper triangle (n(n+1)/2 cells) are freely chosen, each from n values.</desc><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><text x='280' y='30' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>n x n table (here n = 3)</text><rect x='160' y='50' width='50' height='50' class='hi'/><rect x='210' y='50' width='50' height='50' class='hi'/><rect x='260' y='50' width='50' height='50' class='hi'/><rect x='160' y='100' width='50' height='50' class='bg'/><rect x='210' y='100' width='50' height='50' class='hi'/><rect x='260' y='100' width='50' height='50' class='hi'/><rect x='160' y='150' width='50' height='50' class='bg'/><rect x='210' y='150' width='50' height='50' class='bg'/><rect x='260' y='150' width='50' height='50' class='hi'/><text x='340' y='80' class='sub ink'>free cells (choose freely)</text><text x='340' y='130' class='sub mut'>forced by symmetry</text><text x='280' y='235' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ok'>free cells = n(n+1)/2  =>  count = n^(n(n+1)/2)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A 3x3 operation table where the diagonal and upper-triangle cells are highlighted as free choices and the lower triangle is forced by symmetry, giving n(n+1)/2 free cells each with n options.

given.aim: Determine how many distinct commutative binary operations exist on an n-element set.

terms.meaning: the number of elements in the set S
terms.connects: sets the table size to n x n

terms.meaning: an n x n grid whose (i,j) entry is a_i * a_j
terms.example: the cell in row a, column b holds the value a*b
terms.connects: each cell is one independent choice

terms.meaning: a cell on or above the main diagonal whose value we get to pick
terms.example: diagonal cell a*a or upper cell a*b
terms.connects: their count is n + C(n,2) = n(n+1)/2

terms.meaning: each free cell may take any of the n elements as its result
terms.example: a*b could be a, b, or c when n = 3
terms.connects: Product Rule multiplies n across all free cells

given.plan: Count the cells that can be filled freely under commutativity (diagonal plus upper triangle = n(n+1)/2), then by the Product Rule raise the number of choices n to that power.

steps.title: Count the freely chosen cells
steps.formula_raw: free cells = n + C(n,2) = n + n(n-1)/2 = n(n+1)/2
steps.apply: the n diagonal cells (a_i * a_i) plus the C(n,2) upper-triangle cells are free; the lower triangle is forced equal to its mirror by a*b = b*a
steps.note: symmetry across the diagonal removes exactly the C(n,2) lower-triangle cells as independent choices

steps.title: Multiply n choices over all free cells
steps.formula_raw: count = n^(number of free cells) = n^(n(n+1)/2)
steps.apply: each of the n(n+1)/2 free cells independently takes any of n values, so by the Product Rule the total is n raised to that exponent
steps.note: for n = 2 this gives 2^3 = 8; for n = 3 it gives 3^6 = 729

questions.formula_note: A commutative operation has a symmetric table, leaving n(n+1)/2 free cells each with n choices, so the count is n^(n(n+1)/2).

questions.question: On the set of non-negative integers, the binary operation ______ is associative and non-commutative. (Fill in a binary operation that satisfies both properties.)
questions.answer: a * b = a  (left projection); it is associative since (a*b)*c = a = a*(b*c), but non-commutative since a*b = a while b*a = b
questions.to_find: Name a binary operation on non-negative integers that is associative yet not commutative.

understand.plain: We need one concrete operation that is closed on the non-negative integers, obeys the associative law, but fails commutativity. The simplest example is the 'left projection' a*b = a, which always returns its first input. It is associative because both groupings collapse to the leftmost element, and it is non-commutative because swapping the inputs gives a different answer whenever a and b differ. (The 'right projection' a*b = b works the same way.)

keywords.explain: grouping does not change the result: (a*b)*c = a*(b*c) for all a,b,c
order matters: there exist a,b with a*b not equal to b*a
an operation that ignores one input and returns the other, e.g. a*b = a always returns the left operand

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 580 250' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' role='img' aria-label='Left projection is associative but not commutative'><title>Left projection a*b = a</title><desc>The operation a*b = a always returns the first input; it is associative because both groupings give the leftmost value a, but non-commutative because a*b = a differs from b*a = b.</desc><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><text x='290' y='32' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>a * b = a  (return the left operand)</text><rect x='30' y='60' width='250' height='70' rx='10' class='hi'/><text x='155' y='90' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>associative</text><text x='155' y='115' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ok'>(a*b)*c = a = a*(b*c)</text><rect x='300' y='60' width='250' height='70' rx='10' class='bg'/><text x='425' y='90' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>non-commutative</text><text x='425' y='115' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl no'>a*b = a  but  b*a = b</text><text x='290' y='185' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>e.g. (2*5)*7 = 2 = 2*(5*7)</text><text x='290' y='218' text-anchor='middle' class='tag no'>2*5 = 2  while  5*2 = 5</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: The left-projection operation a*b = a is shown to be associative (both groupings yield the leftmost element) yet non-commutative (a*b = a differs from b*a = b).

given.aim: Exhibit a single binary operation on the non-negative integers that is associative but not commutative.

terms.meaning: the set {0, 1, 2, 3, ...} on which the operation must be closed
terms.example: a, b, c are any of 0,1,2,...
terms.connects: left projection stays inside this set

terms.meaning: the operation defined by a * b = a for all a, b
terms.connects: the candidate that we test for both properties

terms.example: (a*b)*c = a*c = a and a*(b*c) = a*b = a
terms.connects: both sides reduce to a, so it holds

terms.meaning: see whether a*b can differ from b*a
terms.example: a*b = a, b*a = b; unequal when a != b

given.plan: Propose a * b = a (left projection), then verify associativity by expanding both groupings and disprove commutativity with a single unequal pair.

steps.title: Check associativity of a * b = a
steps.apply: (a*b)*c = a*c = a  and  a*(b*c) = a*b = a; both groupings equal a, so the law holds for all a,b,c
steps.note: left projection always keeps the leftmost element, so grouping never matters

steps.title: Disprove commutativity
steps.apply: a*b = a but b*a = b; taking a = 2, b = 5 gives 2*5 = 2 while 5*2 = 5, which are different
steps.note: one counterexample is enough to show the operation is non-commutative

solution.result: a * b = a (left projection) is associative and non-commutative; equivalently a * b = b (right projection) also works.

questions.formula_note: The left-projection operation a*b = a is associative (both groupings give the leftmost element) but non-commutative (a*b = a differs from b*a = b).

questions.question: Consider the set {a, b, c} with binary operators + and * defined as follows. For +: a+a=b, a+b=a, a+c=c, b+a=a, b+b=b, b+c=c, c+a=a, c+b=c, c+c=b. For *: a*a=a, a*b=b, a*c=c, b*a=b, b*b=c, b*c=a, c*a=c, c*b=c, c*c=b. For example, a+c=c, c+a=a, c*b=c and b*c=a. Given the following set of equations: (a*x)+(a*y)=c and (b*x)+(c*y)=c. The number of solution(s) (i.e., pairs (x,y) that satisfy the equations) is A. 0  B. 1  C. 2  D. 3
questions.to_find: How many ordered pairs (x, y) from {a,b,c} satisfy both given equations.

understand.plain: Both + and * are given by lookup tables, not by familiar arithmetic, so you simply read off values. There are only 3 choices for x and 3 for y, giving 9 candidate pairs - small enough to test directly. For each pair, compute the two left-hand sides using the tables and keep the pairs where both equal c. Doing this carefully shows exactly two pairs work: (x,y) = (b,c) and (x,y) = (c,b).

keywords.explain: the value of p*q or p+q is read from the row p, column q of the given table - no algebra rules apply
keywords.example: b*b = c straight from the * table

keywords.explain: a solution assigns one element of {a,b,c} to x and one to y; order matters
with only 3 x 3 = 9 possibilities you can test every pair against both equations
keywords.example: list all 9, evaluate, keep those giving c and c

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 600 270' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' role='img' aria-label='Testing the nine candidate pairs'><title>Testing the nine candidate pairs</title><desc>Of the nine ordered pairs (x,y), only (b,c) and (c,b) make both (a*x)+(a*y) and (b*x)+(c*y) equal c, so there are 2 solutions.</desc><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe y='70' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>(a,a)</text><rect x='255' y='45' width='90' height='40' class='bg'/><text x='300' y='70' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>(b,a)</text><rect x='350' y='45' width='90' height='40' class='bg'/><text x='395' y='70' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>(c,a)</text><rect x='160' y='90' width='90' height='40' class='bg'/><text x='205' y='115' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>(a,b)</text><rect x='255' y='90' width='90' height='40' class='bg'/><text x='300' y='115' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>(b,b)</text><rect x='350' y='90' width='90' height='40' class='hi'/><text x='395' y='115' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ok'>(c,b)</text><rect x='160' y='135' width='90' height='40' class='bg'/><text x='205' y='160' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>(a,c)</text><rect x='255' y='135' width='90' height='40' class='hi'/><text x='300' y='160' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ok'>(b,c)</text><rect x='350' y='135' width='90' height='40' class='bg'/><text x='395' y='160' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>(c,c)</text><text x='300' y='212' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ok'>(b,c) and (c,b) satisfy both equations</text><text x='300' y='246' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>number of solutions = 2</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A 3x3 grid of the nine (x,y) candidate pairs with (b,c) and (c,b) highlighted as the two that satisfy both equations.

given.aim: Count the ordered pairs (x,y) over {a,b,c} that satisfy (a*x)+(a*y)=c and (b*x)+(c*y)=c.

terms.meaning: lookup table defining p+q for p,q in {a,b,c}
terms.connects: combines the two products in each equation

terms.meaning: lookup table defining p*q for p,q in {a,b,c}
each equation's right-hand side that both expressions must equal
terms.example: a*b + a*c must come out to c
terms.connects: the acceptance condition for a pair

given.plan: Since x and y each have only 3 values, exhaustively evaluate both left-hand sides from the tables for all 9 pairs and count those that yield c in both equations.

steps.title: Read the inner products from the * table
steps.formula_raw: a*a=a, a*b=b, a*c=c; b*a=b, b*b=c, b*c=a; c*a=c, c*b=c, c*c=b
steps.apply: for each candidate (x,y) look up a*x, a*y for equation 1 and b*x, c*y for equation 2 directly from the * table
steps.note: no algebraic simplification is possible; the tables are the definition

steps.title: Combine with + and test against c for all 9 pairs
steps.apply: (b,c): Eq1 = (a*b)+(a*c) = b+c = c, Eq2 = (b*b)+(c*c) = c+b = c -> both c. (c,b): Eq1 = (a*c)+(a*b) = c+b = c, Eq2 = (b*c)+(c*b) = a+c = c -> both c. Every other pair fails at least one equation
steps.note: the two surviving pairs are (x,y) = (b,c) and (x,y) = (c,b)

questions.formula_note: Pure table lookup over the 9 candidate pairs: only (b,c) and (c,b) make both equations equal c, giving 2 solutions.

questions.question: A logical binary relation $, is defined as follows: A$B is True when (A,B) = (True,True); True when (A,B) = (True,False); False when (A,B) = (False,True); True when (A,B) = (False,False). Let ~ be the unary negation (NOT) operator, with higher precedence than $. Which one of the following is equivalent to A AND B? A. (~A $ B)  B. ~(A $ ~B)  C. ~(~A $ ~B)  D. ~(~A $ B)
questions.to_find: Which expression using the relation $ is logically equivalent to A AND B.

understand.plain: Read the truth table for $: A$B is False only in the single row (A=False, B=True) and True everywhere else. That pattern is exactly 'A OR NOT B', equivalently 'B implies A'. To match A AND B (which is True only when both are True) you negate the right output once and feed in ~A: ~A $ B works out to ~A OR ~B = NOT(A AND B), so negating that whole thing gives back A AND B.

keywords.explain: a row-by-row listing of an operator's output for every combination of inputs
keywords.example: $ is False only for (F,T); True in the other 3 rows

keywords.explain: the single False row (F,T) is the signature of A OR (NOT B), i.e. B -> A
keywords.example: A$B = False exactly when A is False and B is True

keywords.explain: negating a disjunction flips it to a conjunction: ~(P OR Q) = ~P AND ~Q

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 620 270' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' role='img' aria-label='Truth tables for A$B and A AND B'><title>Truth tables for A$B and A AND B</title><desc>A$B is False only in row (F,T); substituting ~A for A makes ~A$B equal NOT(A AND B), and negating that recovers A AND B.</desc><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe ink'>A</text><rect x='80' y='40' width='50' height='34' class='bg'/><text x='105' y='63' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>B</text><rect x='130' y='40' width='60' height='34' class='bg'/><text x='160' y='63' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>A$B</text><text x='55' y='98' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>T</text><text x='105' y='98' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>T</text><text x='160' y='98' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ok'>T</text><text x='55' y='126' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>T</text><text x='105' y='126' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>F</text><text x='160' y='126' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ok'>T</text><text x='55' y='154' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>F</text><text x='105' y='154' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>T</text><text x='160' y='154' text-anchor='middle' class='sub no'>F</text><text x='55' y='182' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>F</text><text x='105' y='182' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>F</text><text x='160' y='182' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ok'>T</text><text x='430' y='28' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>build A AND B</text><rect x='330' y='50' width='240' height='44' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='450' y='78' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>A$B  =  A OR ~B</text><rect x='330' y='104' width='240' height='44' rx='8' class='hi'/><text x='450' y='132' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>~A $ B  =  ~A OR ~B = ~(A AND B)</text><rect x='330' y='158' width='240' height='44' rx='8' class='hi'/><text x='450' y='186' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ok'>~(~A $ B) = A AND B</text><text x='310' y='240' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ok'>Option D:  ~(~A $ B)  =  A AND B</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: The truth table shows A$B is False only for (F,T) so A$B = A OR ~B; substituting ~A gives ~A$B = NOT(A AND B), and its negation ~(~A$B) equals A AND B.

given.aim: Find the option built from $ and ~ that has the same truth table as A AND B.

terms.meaning: binary operator that is False only on (A,B) = (False,True)
terms.example: F $ T = F; all other rows are T

terms.meaning: unary NOT, applied with higher precedence than $
terms.example: ~A flips A's truth value before $ acts
terms.connects: used to reshape the $ output into AND

terms.meaning: the target: True only when both A and B are True
terms.example: T,T -> T; every other row -> F
terms.connects: the truth table each option is compared against

terms.meaning: two expressions match iff they agree on all 4 input rows
terms.example: compare option D's column with A AND B's column

given.plan: Translate $ into a Boolean formula (A OR ~B), substitute ~A for A as each option requires, simplify with De Morgan, and pick the option whose result is A AND B.

steps.title: Express $ as a Boolean formula
steps.formula_raw: A $ B = A OR (~B)   (False only when A=F, B=T)
steps.apply: the truth table is False only in row (F,T), which is precisely the row where A OR ~B fails, so A $ B = A OR ~B
steps.note: equivalently A $ B is the implication B -> A

steps.title: Evaluate option D and confirm it equals A AND B
steps.apply: ~A $ B = (~A) OR ~(B) = ~A OR ~B = ~(A AND B) by De Morgan; negating gives ~(~A $ B) = A AND B. Checking the other options: A.(~A $ B)=~(A AND B), B.~(A $ ~B)= F (always false), C.~(~A $ ~B)=A AND ~B - none match
steps.note: only option D reproduces the A AND B column on all four rows

questions.formula_note: The table makes A $ B = A OR ~B; substituting ~A gives ~A $ B = ~(A AND B), so ~(~A $ B) = A AND B, which is option D.

formulas.name: Number of Commutative Binary Operations on an n-Element Set
formulas.plain: the number of commutative binary operations on a set of n elements is n^(n(n+1)/2), since the symmetric operation table has n(n+1)/2 freely chosen cells (diagonal plus upper triangle) each filled with one of n values
formulas.whenToUse: when counting binary operations on a finite set that must satisfy commutativity (a*b = b*a)

terms.means: number of elements in the set
count of free cells = n diagonal cells + C(n,2) upper-triangle cells

formulas.trap: without the commutativity restriction the count is n^(n^2) (all n^2 cells free); commutativity cuts the free cells to n(n+1)/2, not n^2/2
formulas.reference: Standard counting of operation tables under symmetry; abstract algebra / discrete mathematics

formulas.name: Associativity of a Binary Operation
formulas.plain: a binary operation * is associative when regrouping the operands does not change the result, i.e. (a*b)*c equals a*(b*c) for all elements a, b, c
formulas.whenToUse: to verify or define associativity when testing algebraic structures (semigroups, monoids, groups) or constructing example operations

terms.means: arbitrary elements of the set, used to check both groupings

formulas.trap: associativity is independent of commutativity: an operation can be associative but not commutative (e.g. left projection a*b = a) or commutative but not associative, so always test the two properties separately
formulas.reference: Definition of a semigroup; standard abstract algebra

formulas.name: Logical Equivalence via Truth Tables
formulas.latex: P \equiv Q \iff P \text{ and } Q \text{ share the same truth table}
formulas.plain: two Boolean expressions are logically equivalent exactly when they produce the same output for every assignment of truth values to their variables; a custom operator can be identified by reading off the rows where it is True or False
formulas.whenToUse: to identify or match an unfamiliar logical operator, or to prove two formulas equal, by comparing their output columns row by row

terms.means: Boolean expressions over the same variables
one assignment of True/False to all variables

formulas.trap: remember operator precedence (here ~ binds tighter than $) when parsing an expression, and use De Morgan's laws ~(P OR Q) = ~P AND ~Q to simplify negations of compound expressions
formulas.reference: Propositional logic; truth-table method and De Morgan's laws
